name: Council on Foreign Relations
description: The Council on Foreign Relations (CFR) is an American think tank established in 1921. Its stated mission is to be a resource for its members, government officials, business executives, journalists, educators and students, civic and religious leaders, and other interested citizens in order to help them better understand the world and the foreign policy choices facing the United States and other countries. It achieves this through publications like *Foreign Affairs*, meetings, and studies. Conspiracy theories surrounding the CFR often allege that it is a shadow government or a powerful elite group manipulating global events to establish a New World Order. Proponents point to the CFR's influential membership, which includes prominent figures in government, finance, and media, as evidence of its alleged control. They also cite instances where CFR members have advocated for specific policies that later became government initiatives. Mainstream perspectives view the CFR as a non-partisan organization providing analysis and fostering debate on foreign policy issues. Critics of the conspiracy theories argue that the CFR's influence is often overstated and that attributing global events solely to its actions is an oversimplification of complex geopolitical realities. They emphasize that the CFR's work is transparent and publicly accessible, countering the notion of a secretive cabal. The debate revolves around the extent of the CFR's influence and whether its activities constitute legitimate policy analysis or a more sinister attempt at global control.
subtitle: US foreign policy think tank and conspiracies.

claims.claim: The CFR is a shadow government controlling US foreign policy.
claims.evidence: High-profile members in government and influential positions; advocacy for policies that later become law.
claims.criticism: Membership is diverse and represents a wide range of views; policy recommendations are publicly available and subject to debate.

claims.claim: The CFR is working to establish a New World Order.
claims.evidence: Statements by some members advocating for global cooperation and integration; perceived influence on international organizations.
claims.criticism: Global cooperation is a necessary aspect of modern international relations; there's no evidence of a coordinated plan for global domination.

claims.claim: The CFR manipulates global events for its own benefit.
claims.evidence: Timing of events coinciding with CFR policy recommendations; perceived bias in CFR publications.
claims.criticism: Correlation does not equal causation; CFR's analysis is based on publicly available information and subject to scrutiny.

keyFigures.name: Carroll Quigley
keyFigures.role: Historian whose work is cited by conspiracy theorists.

keyFigures.name: Richard Haass
keyFigures.role: President of the Council on Foreign Relations

mythologyConnections: Illuminati (as a secret society controlling the world)

timeline.event: Publication of the first issue of *Foreign Affairs*.
Increased scrutiny and conspiracy theories emerge regarding the CFR's influence.

sources.author: Shoup, Laurence H.
sources.work: Wall Street's Think Tank: The Council on Foreign Relations and the Making of American Foreign Policy, 1921-1945
sources.title: Shoup, Laurence H.

sources.author: Domhoff, G. William

sources.title: Domhoff, G. William

worshipPractices.name: The Policy Brief Ritual
worshipPractices.description: Devotees meticulously craft policy briefs, offering them as intellectual sacrifices to the gods of influence. These briefs are believed to shape the flow of power and guide the hands of global leaders, ensuring the stability of the world order.

worshipPractices.name: The Roundtable Ceremony
worshipPractices.description: Members gather in circular formations to engage in sacred dialogue, seeking wisdom from diverse perspectives. These discussions are considered a form of collective divination, aimed at predicting and mitigating potential crises on the global stage.

festivals.name: The Annual Conference
festivals.description: A grand convocation of the faithful, where insights are shared, alliances are forged, and the direction of global affairs is debated. It's a time for reaffirming commitment to the ideals of informed leadership and international cooperation.

festivals.name: The New Members Induction
festivals.description: A formal ceremony where new initiates are welcomed into the fold, symbolizing the continuation of the CFR's mission across generations. They are bestowed with the mantle of responsibility for shaping the future.

cultural.modernLegacy: The CFR's influence is evident in countless policy decisions and international agreements, subtly shaping the narratives presented in news media and academic discourse. It serves as a real-world analog to ancient oracles, consulted by those seeking to understand the intricacies of the global landscape and navigate its complexities.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Asgardian Council is formed by the most powerful Aesir to discuss and decide on matters of inter-realm policy, defense, and diplomacy, similar to the CFR's role in global affairs.

cross_cultural_parallels.entity: Council of the Gods on Mount Olympus
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: The Olympian council, led by Zeus, met to deliberate on mortal affairs and intervene in human conflicts, influencing the course of history and maintaining order, mirroring CFR's influence on international relations.

The Ennead, a group of nine powerful deities, convened to establish cosmic order and manage the affairs of the world, influencing pharaohs and determining the fate of Egypt much like the CFR seeks to influence global policy.

associations.name: The Loom of Fate
associations.significance: Like the Fates weaving destiny, the CFR is perceived by some as shaping global events through influence and policy recommendations.

associations.name: Janus
associations.significance: Reflecting Janus, the Roman god of beginnings and transitions, the CFR is associated with shaping new international orders and navigating shifting global landscapes.

associations.name: Seven
associations.significance: Associated with completeness and influence in various mythologies, 'Seven' mirrors the perceived comprehensive reach and impact of the CFR's network and influence.

associations.name: The North Star
associations.significance: Similar to the North Star guiding navigation, the CFR is viewed by some as providing a fixed point of reference and direction for foreign policy decisions.

associations.name: Air
associations.significance: Like the pervasive and unseen element of Air, the CFR's influence is felt globally and permeates various levels of decision-making, often operating behind the scenes.

associations.name: Purple
associations.significance: Associated with royalty and power, the color purple symbolizes the CFR's perceived elite status and influence in international affairs.

associations.name: The Oracle
associations.significance: Similar to an oracle providing guidance, the CFR is consulted for its expertise and insights on complex international issues, shaping policy discussions.

companions.name: Argus Panoptes
companions.description: Argus, the all-seeing giant, would serve as a vigilant protector of the Council's secrets and plans, ensuring no hidden agendas or threats go unnoticed. His hundred eyes provide unparalleled surveillance and insight into global affairs.

companions.name: Hermes/Mercury
companions.description: As the messenger of the gods, Hermes would facilitate communication and diplomacy for the Council, ensuring swift and clear exchanges between nations and leaders. He symbolizes efficient information flow and negotiation.

companions.name: Grímnir (Odin in disguise)
companions.description: Odin, in disguise, would offer strategic advice and wisdom gleaned from centuries of observation and manipulation of mortal affairs. He represents long-term strategic thinking and understanding of power dynamics.

companions.name: The Sphinx
companions.description: The Sphinx, with its enigmatic riddles, would challenge the Council to critically examine their assumptions and policies, ensuring well-reasoned and thoroughly considered decisions. It represents critical thinking and anticipation of unintended consequences.

extendedContent.title: The Oracle of Delphi as Ancient Policy Advisor: A Mythological Precedent
extendedContent.content:
The Oracle of Delphi, priestess of Apollo in ancient Greece, served as a pivotal policy advisor to city-states and individuals alike, embodying a mythological precursor to the Council on Foreign Relations. Functioning from the 8th century BCE to the 4th century CE, the Oracle's pronouncements, often cryptic and open to interpretation, significantly influenced political decisions, warfare strategies, and even colonization efforts. City-states like Athens and Sparta routinely consulted the Oracle before embarking on major initiatives, believing her divine insights were crucial for success. Her influence transcended mere divination; it shaped foreign policy, demonstrating the power of perceived wisdom and foresight in international affairs. 

Plato, in his *Republic*, alludes to the authority of oracular pronouncements in guiding societal structures, mirroring the CFR's modern influence on policy discourse. The ambiguity inherent in the Oracle's pronouncements mirrors the complex and often uncertain nature of international relations, requiring careful analysis and interpretation – a skill valued by both ancient supplicants and modern policymakers. The legend of Croesus, King of Lydia, misinterpreting the Oracle's prophecy about destroying an empire, highlights the dangers of flawed analysis and the importance of accurate understanding in foreign policy decision-making. 

Ultimately, the Oracle of Delphi represents a mythological archetype of a trusted advisor, whose wisdom, derived from a perceived higher source, shaped the course of nations. This parallels the CFR's modern function as a source of expertise and analysis, shaping the discourse surrounding U.S. foreign policy and international relations, albeit through secular means.

extendedContent.title: The Round Table of King Arthur: A Paragon of Collaborative Governance
extendedContent.content:
The Round Table of King Arthur, as depicted in Arthurian legends, offers a compelling mythological analogue to the Council on Foreign Relations, representing a collaborative body dedicated to justice, diplomacy, and the well-being of a realm. The very shape of the table, round and without hierarchical placement, symbolized equality and the importance of diverse perspectives in decision-making. Arthur's knights, representing different regions and backgrounds, convened regularly to discuss matters of state, address grievances, and strategize against external threats. This collaborative approach mirrors the CFR's emphasis on bringing together experts from various fields to analyze complex global issues and formulate policy recommendations.

Sir Thomas Malory's *Le Morte d'Arthur* provides detailed accounts of the Round Table's deliberations, showcasing the importance of debate, compromise, and shared responsibility in governance. The quests undertaken by the knights, often involving diplomatic missions and conflict resolution, can be seen as mythological representations of foreign policy initiatives. The failures of the Round Table, such as the internal strife and eventual dissolution due to betrayal and the pursuit of the Holy Grail, serve as cautionary tales about the fragility of collaborative governance and the importance of maintaining ethical standards.

The Arthurian legend, therefore, presents a compelling model of a council dedicated to shaping a realm's destiny through collaborative deliberation and strategic action. While idealized and fantastical, the Round Table embodies principles of inclusivity, diplomacy, and shared responsibility that resonate with the CFR's contemporary mission of fostering informed foreign policy discourse.

extendedContent.title: The Norse Allthing: A Mythological Assembly for Foreign Affairs
extendedContent.content:
In Norse mythology, the Allthing (Alþingi) served as a paramount assembly where free men gathered to legislate, administer justice, and address matters of both domestic and foreign significance. This assembly, depicted in sagas like the *Icelandic Sagas* and the *Prose Edda*, offers a parallel to the Council on Foreign Relations, showcasing a forum for discussion and decision-making concerning international relations. While the Allthing primarily dealt with legal matters and internal disputes, it also played a crucial role in resolving conflicts with other tribes and kingdoms, negotiating trade agreements, and declaring war. These functions directly relate to the core concerns of foreign policy.

The sagas often depict the Allthing as a place of intense debate and negotiation, where powerful chieftains vied for influence and sought to advance their interests. This reflects the complex and often contentious nature of international relations, where different actors with competing agendas must find common ground. The Lawspeaker, a key figure in the Allthing, played a role akin to a neutral mediator, guiding discussions and ensuring that decisions were made in accordance with established laws and customs. This echoes the CFR's role in facilitating dialogue and promoting informed debate on foreign policy issues.

While the Allthing was not a formal council in the same sense as the CFR, it served as a vital forum for addressing issues of international significance in the Norse world. It highlights the importance of open dialogue, legal frameworks, and diplomatic engagement in managing relations between different groups and nations. The Allthing, therefore, provides a valuable mythological perspective on the challenges and opportunities of foreign policy decision-making.

extendedContent.title: The Egyptian Ennead: Divine Council and the Shaping of Cosmic Order
extendedContent.content:
The Ennead, the council of nine principal deities in ancient Egyptian mythology, presents a cosmological model for understanding the formation and maintenance of order, both within Egypt and in its interactions with the wider world. Led by Atum (or Ra in later traditions), the Ennead comprised gods and goddesses representing fundamental aspects of existence, including creation, justice, and the natural world. Their collective actions and pronouncements, as depicted in texts like the *Pyramid Texts* and the *Book of the Dead*, shaped the very fabric of reality, establishing principles of governance and ensuring the stability of the cosmos. The Pharaoh, as the earthly representative of the gods, was expected to uphold these principles, ensuring harmony within Egypt and maintaining its position in the world.

While the Ennead primarily focused on cosmic order, its implications for foreign relations are significant. Maintaining Ma'at (truth, justice, and cosmic balance) was crucial not only for internal stability but also for ensuring favorable relationships with neighboring lands. Treaties, trade agreements, and military campaigns were all conducted with the aim of upholding Ma'at and projecting Egyptian power and influence. The Ennead's role in establishing these principles can be seen as a mythological analogue to the CFR's role in shaping the discourse surrounding U.S. foreign policy and promoting a particular vision of international order.

Furthermore, the myths surrounding the Ennead often involve conflicts with forces of chaos and disorder, both internal and external. These conflicts represent the challenges of maintaining stability and security in a world fraught with threats. The Ennead's constant vigilance and intervention underscore the importance of proactive engagement in addressing potential threats and safeguarding national interests, a concept that resonates with contemporary foreign policy debates.

extendedContent.title: The Iroquois Grand Council: A Model of Diplomacy and Confederation
extendedContent.content:
The Iroquois Grand Council, or Haudenosaunee Confederacy, represents a powerful example of a functioning council dedicated to diplomacy, conflict resolution, and the collective governance of multiple nations. This historical and culturally significant confederation, formed centuries before European contact, united the Mohawk, Oneida, Onondaga, Cayuga, and Seneca nations (later joined by the Tuscarora) under the Great Law of Peace. The Grand Council, composed of representatives from each nation, met regularly to discuss matters of common concern, negotiate treaties with other tribes and European powers, and maintain peace among its member nations.

Oral tradition and historical accounts detail the intricate protocols and procedures of the Grand Council, emphasizing the importance of consensus-building, respectful dialogue, and adherence to the Great Law of Peace. Decisions were made through careful deliberation and unanimous agreement, reflecting a commitment to shared governance and the avoidance of unilateral action. The Grand Council's success in maintaining peace and stability among its member nations for centuries stands as a testament to the effectiveness of its diplomatic approach and its commitment to collaborative decision-making.

The Iroquois Confederacy's influence extended beyond its immediate borders, shaping the political landscape of North America and serving as a model for other indigenous groups. Some scholars even argue that the Iroquois Confederacy influenced the drafting of the Articles of Confederation and the U.S. Constitution. The Iroquois Grand Council, therefore, provides a compelling historical and cultural example of a functioning council dedicated to diplomacy, conflict resolution, and the promotion of peace and stability, offering valuable insights for contemporary organizations like the CFR.
